searchDocs.title: General FAQ
searchDocs.keywords: QA ZERO  WordPress  analytics  heatmap  documentation

searchDocs.title: What is QA ZERO?​
searchDocs.content: QA ZERO is a WordPress plugin that provides analytics, heatmaps, and session recording functionality to help you understand how users interact with your website.

searchDocs.title: How does QA ZERO compare to Google Analytics?​
searchDocs.content: While Google Analytics provides comprehensive traffic data, QA ZERO focuses on user behavior and interaction. QA ZERO offers visual tools like heatmaps and session recordings that Google Analytics doesn't provide. Many users use both tools together for a complete picture of their website performance.

searchDocs.title: Does QA ZERO slow down my website?​
searchDocs.content: QA ZERO is designed to have minimal impact on your website's performance. The tracking script is lightweight and loads asynchronously, meaning it doesn't block your page from loading.

searchDocs.title: Is QA ZERO GDPR compliant?​
searchDocs.content: Yes, QA ZERO includes features to help you comply with GDPR and other privacy regulations, including:  Cookie consent integrationData anonymization optionsTools for handling data subject requestsData retention controls

searchDocs.title: DNS Setup Instructions for docs.qazero.com
searchDocs.keywords: QA ZERO  WordPress  analytics  heatmap  documentation

searchDocs.title: Prerequisites​
searchDocs.content: Access to your domain's DNS settings (typically through your domain registrar)GitHub Pages site already deployed

searchDocs.title: 1. Configure GitHub Pages​
searchDocs.content: The repository is already configured to use the custom domain through:  A CNAME file in the repository with docs.qazero.comGitHub Pages settings in the repository settings

searchDocs.title: 2. DNS Configuration​
searchDocs.content: To point your domain to GitHub Pages, you need to add the following DNS records:  Option 1: Apex Domain Configuration (Recommended)​  If you're using the apex domain (docs.qazero.com), add these A records:  Type	Host	Value	TTLA	@	185.199.108.153	3600 A	@	185.199.109.153	3600 A	@	185.199.110.153	3600 A	@	185.199.111.153	3600  Option 2: Subdomain Configuration​  If docs.qazero.com is a subdomain, add a CNAME record:  Type	Host	Value	TTLCNAME	docs	quarka-org.github.io	3600

searchDocs.title: 3. Verify DNS Configuration​
searchDocs.content: After configuring your DNS records:  Wait for DNS propagation (can take up to 48 hours, but often completes within a few hours)Verify the configuration using: dig docs.qazero.com +noall +answer Check that the site is accessible at https://docs.qazero.com

searchDocs.title: 4. Enforce HTTPS​
searchDocs.content: Once the DNS is properly configured:  Go to the GitHub repository settingsNavigate to Pages settingsCheck the "Enforce HTTPS" option

searchDocs.title: Troubleshooting​
searchDocs.content: If the domain is not working correctly:  Verify DNS records are correctly configuredCheck that the CNAME file exists in the repositoryEnsure GitHub Pages is enabled in repository settingsCheck for any error messages in the GitHub Pages section of repository settings

searchDocs.title: Getting Started​
searchDocs.content: Get started by creating a new site.  Or try Docusaurus immediately with docusaurus.new.

searchDocs.title: What you'll need​
searchDocs.content: Node.js version 18.0 or above: When installing Node.js, you are recommended to check all checkboxes related to dependencies.

searchDocs.title: Generate a new site​
searchDocs.content: Generate a new Docusaurus site using the classic template.  The classic template will automatically be added to your project after you run the command:  npm init docusaurus@latest my-website classic   You can type this command into Command Prompt, Powershell, Terminal, or any other integrated terminal of your code editor.  The command also installs all necessary dependencies you need to run Docusaurus.

searchDocs.title: Start your site​
searchDocs.content: Run the development server:  cd my-website npm run start   The cd command changes the directory you're working with. In order to work with your newly created Docusaurus site, you'll need to navigate the terminal there.  The npm run start command builds your website locally and serves it through a development server, ready for you to view at http://localhost:3000/.  Open docs/intro.md (this page) and edit some lines: the site reloads automatically and displays your changes.

searchDocs.title: Search Implementation Security Review
searchDocs.keywords: QA ZERO  WordPress  analytics  heatmap  documentation

searchDocs.title: Implementation Choice​
searchDocs.content: For the QA ZERO documentation site, we have implemented the docusaurus-lunr-search plugin (version 3.6.0+) as the primary search solution. This choice was made based on the following security considerations:

searchDocs.title: Security Considerations​
searchDocs.content: Client-side Search: The plugin performs all search operations on the client side, eliminating the need for external API calls or server-side processing. No External Dependencies: The search functionality does not rely on external services, reducing potential attack vectors and data leakage risks. No Data Collection: Unlike some search solutions that send queries to external servers, docusaurus-lunr-search keeps all search queries and indexes local to the user's browser. Open Source: The code is open source and has been reviewed by the community, increasing the likelihood that security issues are identified and addressed. Minimal JavaScript: The plugin uses a minimal amount of JavaScript, reducing the potential attack surface.

searchDocs.title: Code Audit Results​
searchDocs.content: We conducted a thorough code audit of the docusaurus-lunr-search plugin with the following findings:  No Suspicious Code: No suspicious or malicious code was identified in the plugin. No Unnecessary Network Requests: The plugin does not make any unnecessary network requests or transmit search data to external servers. No Sensitive Data Handling: The plugin does not handle or store sensitive user data. Proper Input Sanitization: The plugin properly sanitizes search inputs to prevent XSS attacks.

searchDocs.title: Version Management​
searchDocs.content: To ensure security and stability:  Fixed Version: The plugin is locked to a specific version in package.json to prevent automatic updates that might introduce security vulnerabilities. Regular Updates: A process is in place to regularly review updates to the plugin for security improvements while maintaining version control.

searchDocs.title: Multilingual Support​
searchDocs.content: The plugin has been configured to support both English and Japanese search, with proper handling of Japanese characters and tokenization.

searchDocs.title: Option 2: Basic Search Functionality​
searchDocs.content: The built-in basic search functionality of Docusaurus was considered as a fallback option. While secure, it offers limited functionality compared to the chosen solution.

searchDocs.title: Option 3: @easyops-cn/docusaurus-search-local​
searchDocs.content: This plugin was evaluated but not selected due to security concerns:  Less active maintenanceMore complex codebase with potential security issuesPotential for unnecessary external communications

searchDocs.title: Conclusion​
searchDocs.content: Based on our security review, docusaurus-lunr-search provides the best balance of functionality and security for the QA ZERO documentation site. The plugin operates entirely client-side, does not transmit data to external servers, and has been locked to a specific version to prevent security regressions from automatic updates.

searchDocs.title: Future Improvements​
searchDocs.content: Potential future improvements to search security include:  Regular security audits of the plugin as new versions are releasedConsideration of implementing Content Security Policy (CSP) headers to further restrict potential script executionMonitoring for any security advisories related to the Lunr.js library that powers the search functionality

searchDocs.title: Create your first Doc​
searchDocs.content: Create a Markdown file at docs/hello.md:  docs/hello.md # Hello This is my **first Docusaurus document**!   A new document is now available at http://localhost:3000/docs/hello.

searchDocs.title: Configure the Sidebar​
searchDocs.content: Docusaurus automatically creates a sidebar from the docs folder.  Add metadata to customize the sidebar label and position:  docs/hello.md --- sidebar_label: 'Hi!' sidebar_position: 3 --- # Hello This is my **first Docusaurus document**!   It is also possible to create your sidebar explicitly in sidebars.js:  sidebars.js export default { tutorialSidebar: [ 'intro', 'hello', { type: 'category', label: 'Tutorial', items: ['tutorial-basics/create-a-document'], }, ], };

searchDocs.title: Create your first React Page​
searchDocs.content: Create a file at src/pages/my-react-page.js:  src/pages/my-react-page.js import React from 'react'; import Layout from '@theme/Layout'; export default function MyReactPage() { return ( <Layout> <h1>My React page</h1> <p>This is a React page</p> </Layout> ); }   A new page is now available at http://localhost:3000/my-react-page.

searchDocs.title: Create your first Markdown Page​
searchDocs.content: Create a file at src/pages/my-markdown-page.md:  src/pages/my-markdown-page.md # My Markdown page This is a Markdown page   A new page is now available at http://localhost:3000/my-markdown-page.

searchDocs.title: Analysis Tools​
searchDocs.content: QA ZERO provides powerful tools for analyzing session recordings:  Filtering Options: Filter recordings by: Date rangeDevice typeTraffic sourceGoal completionPage URLSession duration Integration with Other Features: View heatmaps for the same pageAnalyze conversion pathsIdentify usability issuesCorrelate with analytics data

searchDocs.title: Privacy Considerations​

searchDocs.title: About QA ZERO​
searchDocs.content: QA ZERO is a privacy-focused, independent analytics server solution.  Although it is distributed as a WordPress plugin, it is not a typical WordPress extension. WordPress is used as an installation and management interface, while the analytics engine operates independently.  As a result, certain architectural and operational aspects differ from standard WordPress plugins.    This manual serves as a practical guide for safely and effectively operating QA ZERO.  Please refer to the relevant section as needed.

searchDocs.title: Frequently Asked Questions (Start Guide)
searchDocs.keywords: QA ZERO  WordPress  analytics  heatmap  documentation

searchDocs.title: Is QA ZERO just a WordPress plugin?​
searchDocs.content: No. QA ZERO is an independent analytics infrastructure that happens to use WordPress only as a middleware layer for setup and system management. It is not a WordPress CMS plugin in the traditional sense — it does not interact with posts, pages, or themes as content. Instead, it uses WordPress purely as a lightweight UI and plugin framework.

searchDocs.title: Why does QA ZERO use WordPress?​
searchDocs.content: WordPress is used exclusively as a familiar and extensible runtime environment. QA ZERO leverages WordPress's plugin system and admin interface for easy deployment and operation, but all analytics processing, data handling, and visualization are entirely independent from WordPress’s content features. Think of QA ZERO as a stand-alone server app that simply uses WordPress to "host" its management UI.

searchDocs.title: Is QA ZERO compatible with my WordPress theme?​
searchDocs.content: Theme compatibility is irrelevant. QA ZERO does not render or affect your WordPress site's front-end. Since it operates independently from WordPress's public pages and templates, your theme has no impact on its functionality. The plugin runs as a private analytics interface within the admin dashboard.

searchDocs.title: Will QA ZERO slow down my website?​
searchDocs.content: No. QA ZERO is optimized for performance. The tracking script loads asynchronously and does not block rendering. All analytics data is processed server-side in a way that is completely detached from your website's core operation.

searchDocs.title: Can I use QA ZERO with a caching plugin?​
searchDocs.content: Yes. Since QA ZERO doesn't modify your visible site pages, it is compatible with most caching plugins. However, make sure the tracking script is excluded from page caching if needed.

searchDocs.title: How long does it take to start seeing data?​
searchDocs.content: Tracking begins as soon as the script is active on your site. Most users see data in the dashboard within a few minutes. For more comprehensive results like heatmaps, we recommend letting QA ZERO collect data for at least 24–48 hours.

searchDocs.title: Can I exclude certain users from being tracked?​
searchDocs.content: Yes. You can exclude logged-in users, specific user roles, or IP addresses using the built-in settings in the QA ZERO admin panel.

searchDocs.title: Do I need to add any code to my website?​
searchDocs.content: No manual code insertion is required. QA ZERO automatically injects the tracking script into your site's pages once activated.

searchDocs.title: Is QA ZERO GDPR compliant?​
searchDocs.content: Yes. QA ZERO is built with privacy in mind. It includes several tools to support GDPR and other privacy frameworks:  No reliance on cookiesIP address anonymizationConfigurable data retention periodsExport and deletion tools for user dataRespect for opt-out signals from consent tools

searchDocs.title: Does QA ZERO use cookies?​
searchDocs.content: No. QA ZERO is designed to be 100% cookieless. It uses privacy-friendly fingerprinting and session correlation techniques that do not store data on the user’s device.

searchDocs.title: Can visitors opt out of tracking?​
searchDocs.content: Yes. QA ZERO respects signals from consent tools (such as CookieYes) and disables tracking accordingly. If a visitor opts out, no data is recorded.

searchDocs.title: Heatmap Data Aggregation
searchDocs.keywords: QA ZERO  WordPress  analytics  heatmap  documentation

searchDocs.title: Q. Why does the “Sessions” count on the Goal screen not match the “Data Count” on the Heatmap screen?​
searchDocs.content: Example: The Goal screen shows 400 sessions, while the Heatmap shows 1200 data entries.

searchDocs.title: A. Because the counting units are different.​
What “Sessions” on the Goal screen actually represents​
searchDocs.content: The “Sessions” value displayed in the Goal table actually represents:  The total number of page views (PV) for which heatmap data was recorded.  Although the label says “Sessions,” the internal logic aggregates valid heatmap-related page views (PV).

searchDocs.title: What “Data Count” on the Heatmap screen represents​
searchDocs.content: The “Data Count” displayed on the Heatmap screen also counts data in page view (PV) units.  However, it may appear larger than the Goal screen value for the following reasons:  Multiple views within a single session If one user (one session) views the target page three times (3 PV), the session count is “1,” but the heatmap data is recorded as “3.” Label definition differences The Goal table uses the label “Sessions” for convenience, but internally aggregates PV counts per device. Therefore, the value tends to be closer to PV totals rather than actual session counts.

searchDocs.title: Q. Even if the numbers differ, is the heatmap drawn only from sessions that achieved the goal?​
A. Yes. The data is correctly filtered.​
searchDocs.content: The heatmap rendering process extracts:  Only data associated with sessions that achieved the goal.  The numerical difference occurs because:  Actions after goal completionRepeated views within the same session  are all counted in PV units.  Data from users who did not achieve the goal is not included in the heatmap.

searchDocs.title: How to Interpret the Numbers (Summary)​
searchDocs.content: Screen	Field Name	What the Number RepresentsGoal screen table	Sessions	Total valid PV count for heatmap data (labelled as Sessions) Heatmap display	Data Count	Total valid log entries used for rendering (PV-based)    💡 Key Point A difference such as 400 (Goal screen) and 1200 (Heatmap) indicates that users who achieved the goal repeatedly viewed or navigated the page. This behavior is correctly reflected in the heatmap data.

searchDocs.title: TSV Report Fields
searchDocs.keywords: QA ZERO  WordPress  analytics  heatmap  documentation

searchDocs.title: ■ pv​
searchDocs.content: Indicates the page view order within a session.  Increments as 1, 2, 3, ...Resets to 1 when a new session begins

searchDocs.title: ■ speed_msec​
searchDocs.content: Page load time in milliseconds.  It represents the time required for the page to fully load in the browser.

searchDocs.title: ■ browse_sec​
searchDocs.content: Time spent on the page (in seconds).  It represents the duration from when the page was opened until the user navigates to the next page.

searchDocs.title: ■ is_last​
searchDocs.content: Indicates whether the page is the exit page of the session.  1 = The last page viewed in the session0 = Otherwise

searchDocs.title: ■ is_newuser​
searchDocs.content: Indicates whether the user is a new user.  1 = New user0 = Returning user  This value is determined per session, so all rows within the same session will have the same value.

searchDocs.title: ■ session_no​
searchDocs.content: Indicates the visit number of the user for that day.  In QA ZERO:  A session ends if there is no activity for 30 minutes.  If more than 30 minutes pass between interactions, a new session is created and session_no increases.

searchDocs.title: When reader_id Is the Same but session_no Is Different​
searchDocs.content: Example: reader_id = 4313  For this user:  session_no = 1 (05:07) → Visited only the top page and exitedsession_no = 2 (08:05) → Revisited approximately 3 hours latersession_no = 3 (19:09) → Revisited approximately 11 hours later  Although the reader_id is the same, each visit is recorded as a separate session because the interval between visits exceeded 30 minutes.

searchDocs.title: Session Boundary Rules​
searchDocs.content: QA ZERO applies the following session rule:  If there is no activity for 30 minutes, the session is considered ended.  This is a commonly used rule in web analytics tools.

searchDocs.title: Summary​
searchDocs.content: pv indicates the page order within a sessionsession_no indicates the visit countSessions are separated by 30 minutes of inactivityis_last marks the exit pageis_newuser is determined per session

searchDocs.title: Complete Data Loss​
searchDocs.content: Complete data loss occurs when all historical data disappears from your QA ZERO dashboard.  Possible causes:  Database Reset: Accidental use of the "Reset Data" functionPlugin Reinstallation: Uninstalling and reinstalling the plugin without proper backupDatabase Corruption: Server issues affecting the WordPress database

searchDocs.title: Partial Data Loss​
searchDocs.content: Partial data loss occurs when some data is missing for specific time periods or pages.  Possible causes:  Tracking Interruption: Temporary issues with the tracking scriptData Processing Errors: Background processing failuresData Retention Settings: Automatic deletion of older dataContent Filtering: Exclusion rules preventing data collection

searchDocs.title: Check Data Collection Status​
searchDocs.content: Navigate to QA ZERO > DashboardCheck the dashboard for recent dataIf recent data is visible, data collection is currently working

searchDocs.title: Verify Recent Data​
searchDocs.content: Navigate to QA ZERO > UserSet the date range to "Today" or "Yesterday"Check if recent data is being recordedIf recent data exists but historical data is missing, the issue is likely with stored data rather than collection

searchDocs.title: Check WordPress Database​
searchDocs.content: Use a database management tool like phpMyAdminCheck the WordPress database tables related to QA ZEROVerify that the tables exist and contain data

searchDocs.title: Review WordPress Error Logs​
searchDocs.content: Check your WordPress error logsLook for errors related to QA ZERONote the timestamps of any relevant errors

searchDocs.title: Data Retention Settings​
searchDocs.content: QA ZERO automatically removes old data based on your retention settings:  Navigate to QA ZERO > SettingsSelect the Site Management tabCheck the Data Retention Period settingIf set to a short period (e.g., 30 days), older data will be automatically deletedAdjust the setting to a longer period if neededNote that changing this setting will not restore already deleted data

searchDocs.title: Database Issues​
searchDocs.content: Database corruption can cause data loss:  Use a database management tool like phpMyAdminCheck the WordPress database tables related to QA ZERORepair or optimize the database tables if needed  For severe database issues:  Check your server's error logs for database-related errorsContact your hosting provider for assistance with database repairRestore from a backup if available

searchDocs.title: Tracking Script Issues​
searchDocs.content: If the tracking script was temporarily disabled or broken:  Navigate to QA ZERO > TagVerify that the tracking code is properly installedIf using a caching plugin, clear your cacheCheck your site again to see if tracking is working

searchDocs.title: Plugin Conflicts​
searchDocs.content: Other plugins may interfere with QA ZERO's data collection or storage:  Temporarily deactivate other plugins one by oneAfter each deactivation, check if QA ZERO data collection worksIf you identify a conflicting plugin, check the Plugin Compatibility section for specific guidance

searchDocs.title: Server Resource Limitations​
searchDocs.content: Limited server resources can prevent data processing:  Check your server's PHP memory limit and execution timeEnsure your server meets the minimum requirements (PHP 5.6+, 64MB memory)If resources are limited, contact your hosting provider about upgrading

searchDocs.title: From Backup​
searchDocs.content: If you have a backup of your WordPress database:  Restore the database from backupVerify that QA ZERO tables are included in the backupReactivate the plugin if necessary

searchDocs.title: Using WordPress Database Tools​
searchDocs.content: If you need to recover data from a corrupted database:  Use a database management tool like phpMyAdminCheck the WordPress database tables related to QA ZERORepair or optimize the database tables if neededIf necessary, consult with a database administrator

searchDocs.title: From Browser Cache​
searchDocs.content: In some cases, limited data may still exist in visitors' browsers:  Note that QA ZERO uses localStorage for some temporary dataThis data is limited and may not represent a complete recovery solutionFor reliable data recovery, always maintain regular database backups

searchDocs.title: Regular Backups​
searchDocs.content: Set up regular database backups:  Use a WordPress backup plugin or your hosting provider's backup toolsEnsure that database backups include QA ZERO tablesConfigure backup frequency and retentionStore backups in a secure location (local or cloud)

searchDocs.title: Optimize Server Resources​
searchDocs.content: Reduce the risk of data processing failures:  Ensure your server meets the minimum requirements (PHP 5.6+, 64MB memory)Consider upgrading your hosting plan if you have a high-traffic siteRegularly check server logs for errors or warningsKeep WordPress and all plugins updated

searchDocs.title: Monitor Site Performance​
searchDocs.content: Monitor your site's performance:  Regularly check the QA ZERO dashboard for any anomaliesUse WordPress health check tools to monitor overall site healthSet up uptime monitoring for your websiteRegularly check for plugin conflicts

searchDocs.title: Getting Support​
searchDocs.content: If you've tried the troubleshooting steps above and still have issues:  Gather information about your system: WordPress versionPHP versionServer environmentQA ZERO plugin version Contact QA ZERO support with: Your system informationDescription of the data lossTime period affectedSteps you've already taken to troubleshoot

searchDocs.title: Troubleshooting Recording Issues
searchDocs.keywords: QA ZERO  WordPress  analytics  heatmap  documentation

searchDocs.title: No Data Being Recorded​
searchDocs.content: If you're not seeing any data in your QA ZERO dashboard:  Verify Tracking is Enabled​  Navigate to QA ZERO > SettingsSelect the Site Management tabEnsure tracking options are enabledSave changes if needed  Check Script Installation​  Visit your website in an incognito/private browsing windowRight-click and select "View Page Source"Search for "qa-zero" in the source codeVerify the tracking script is present in the <head> or before the closing </body> tag    If the script is missing:  Navigate to QA ZERO > TagVerify that the tracking code is properly installedIf using a caching plugin, clear your cacheCheck your site again  Check for JavaScript Errors​  Open your website in Chrome or FirefoxOpen the browser's developer tools (F12 or right-click > Inspect)Go to the Console tabLook for any errors related to QA ZERO

searchDocs.title: Partial Data Recording​
searchDocs.content: If some data is being recorded but not all:  Check Tracking Configuration​  Navigate to QA ZERO > SettingsSelect the Site Management tabVerify that all desired tracking options are enabledSave changes if needed  Check Site Configuration​  Navigate to QA ZERO > SettingsReview the settings in all tabs to ensure proper configurationMake sure your site type and attributes are correctly setSave changes if needed

searchDocs.title: Heatmap Not Displaying Correctly​
searchDocs.content: If heatmaps are not displaying correctly on your pages:  Check Page Compatibility​  Navigate to QA ZERO > BehaviorSelect the page with the problematic heatmapCheck if the page content has changed significantly since data collectionIf needed, clear old data and collect new heatmap data  Adjust Browser View​  Navigate to QA ZERO > BehaviorSelect the page with the problematic heatmapTry switching between device views (desktop, tablet, mobile)Check if the heatmap displays correctly in different views

searchDocs.title: Chrome​
searchDocs.content: If recording issues occur specifically in Chrome:  Check if any ad blockers or privacy extensions are installedVerify that third-party cookies are allowedTry disabling any content blocking features

searchDocs.title: Safari​
searchDocs.content: Safari's Intelligent Tracking Prevention (ITP) can affect analytics:  QA ZERO is designed to work with Safari's privacy featuresNo special configuration is typically neededIf issues persist, check for browser-specific console errors

searchDocs.title: Firefox​
searchDocs.content: If recording issues occur specifically in Firefox:  Check if Enhanced Tracking Protection is blocking QA ZEROQA ZERO is designed to work with Firefox's privacy featuresIf issues persist, check for browser-specific console errors

searchDocs.title: Plugin Conflict Resolution​
searchDocs.content: If you suspect a plugin conflict:  Temporarily deactivate other plugins one by oneAfter each deactivation, check if QA ZERO recording worksIf you identify a conflicting plugin, check the Plugin Compatibility section for specific guidance

searchDocs.title: PHP Settings​
searchDocs.content: Ensure your server meets these requirements:  PHP 5.6 or higherMemory limit: 64MB or higherMax execution time: 30 seconds or higherPOST max size: 8MB or higher

searchDocs.title: WordPress Configuration​
searchDocs.content: Check these WordPress settings:  Ensure WordPress address (URL) and Site address (URL) are correctly set in Settings > GeneralVerify that permalink settings are configured and working

searchDocs.title: Manual Script Verification​
searchDocs.content: Add this code to your theme's header.php file (before the closing </head> tag) to force the tracking script:  <?php if (function_exists('qahm_zero_print_tracking_code')) { qahm_zero_print_tracking_code(); } ?>

searchDocs.title: Database Verification​
searchDocs.content: Use a database management tool like phpMyAdminCheck the WordPress database tables related to QA ZEROVerify that the tables exist and contain dataIf issues are found, consider reinstalling the plugin

searchDocs.title: Getting Support​
searchDocs.content: If you've tried the troubleshooting steps above and still have issues:  Gather information about your system: WordPress versionPHP versionServer environmentBrowser and device information Contact QA ZERO support with: Your system informationDescription of the issueSteps you've already taken to troubleshootScreenshots of any error messages

searchDocs.title: Overview​
searchDocs.content: You can check overall site performance for the selected period. Click the date area at the top of the screen to change the period.  The following metrics are displayed as graphs and values:  Total UsersSessionsPageviews (PV)

searchDocs.title: Users / Devices​
searchDocs.content: You can analyze user activity and goal performance by User Type and Device Category.  User Type  New Visitor: First-time visitorsReturning Visitor: Visitors who have been to the site before  Device Category  dsk: Desktoptab: Tabletsmp: Smartphone

searchDocs.title: Session Recording (Video Playback)​
searchDocs.content: A list of user activity data is displayed for the selected period. Click the icon in the Playback column to watch the session as a video.  You can review detailed behavior such as:  Mouse movementsPage navigation flow

searchDocs.title: Advanced Export​
searchDocs.content: You can download raw data in TSV format for detailed analysis.  The exported data includes URLs with parameters.

searchDocs.title: Examples of Assistants​
searchDocs.content: Multiple assistants are available. Each one focuses on a specific purpose and provides targeted insights.  Here are some examples:

searchDocs.title: Trending Pages Analysis​
searchDocs.content: Identifies pages with rapidly increasing traffic. Useful for spotting trends and finding ideas for new content.

searchDocs.title: SNS Advisor​
searchDocs.content: Analyzes traffic from social media such as X, Instagram, and Facebook. Helps you evaluate SNS performance and increase traffic.

searchDocs.title: Basic Usage​
searchDocs.content: Click AI Assistants in the left menu.Select the assistant you want to use.Answer the prompts (such as selecting a period), and the analysis results and suggestions will be displayed.  To switch assistants, use -- Select AI Assistant -- at the bottom right of the screen.

searchDocs.title: Working with Data (Filter & Export)​
searchDocs.content: You can process and save the data displayed during the conversation.  Filter: Narrow down data by keywords or valuesExport: Download data in CSV or JSON format for further use

searchDocs.title: Example Workflows​
searchDocs.content: The conversation flow and analysis vary depending on the assistant. Below are examples from the initial assistants.

searchDocs.title: Trending Pages Analysis​
searchDocs.content: Identifies landing pages (LPs) that are currently growing.  Select a period:  This week vs last weekLast 28 daysLast 90 days  You can then view the top growing landing pages.  Definition of Change This analysis compares the first 7 days and the last 7 days within the selected period. For more flexible period settings, use Rising Pages in the Behavior report.  Click Analyze top pages in detail to see traffic sources for the top pages (up to 10). This helps you identify the next actions to take.

searchDocs.title: SNS Advisor​
searchDocs.content: Analyzes traffic from social media in detail.  Select a period:  Last 7 daysLast 14 daysLast 28 daysLast 90 days  You can view traffic by referrer and medium.  Then select a specific SNS to see which landing pages users visited. Click Social Media Search Result in the table to view actual search results on SNS (not supported for all platforms).  You can switch SNS to compare results.

searchDocs.title: Heatmap
searchDocs.keywords: QA ZERO  WordPress  analytics  heatmap  documentation

searchDocs.title: How to View a Heatmap​
searchDocs.content: Heatmaps can be accessed from the page reports under Behavior in the left menu.  Open a report for a page and use the Heatmap column in each row to display the heatmap. The data shown reflects the currently selected period and filter conditions.

searchDocs.title: Types of Heatmaps​
searchDocs.content: You can switch between heatmap types from the options at the top of the screen.  The colors and intensity represent the relative concentration of user actions on the page.

searchDocs.title: Scroll Map​
searchDocs.content: The Scroll Map shows how far users scroll on a page.  It indicates what percentage of visitors reached each position on the page. The number of users who reached that position is also displayed.

searchDocs.title: Attention Map​
searchDocs.content: The Attention Map highlights areas where users tend to focus their attention.  Sections that are viewed more frequently appear in red. This is calculated based on viewing time and dwell patterns.

searchDocs.title: Click Heatmap​
searchDocs.content: The Click Heatmap visualizes where clicks occurred on the page.  Areas with more clicks appear redder. Clicks on non-link elements are also included.

searchDocs.title: Click Count Map​
searchDocs.content: The Click Count Map displays the number of clicks for each element.  It shows how many times banners, buttons, or links were clicked.  In this map, the background color of the click count depends on the CSS positioning of the element.  Blue background Indicates elements that stay fixed on the screen when scrolling. These are elements whose HTML tag or parent element uses position: absolute or position: fixed. Black background Indicates elements positioned within the normal page layout.

searchDocs.title: Color Scale​
searchDocs.content: In the Scroll Map, Attention Map, and Click Heatmap, colors change gradually to represent the intensity of user activity.

searchDocs.title: Screen Controls​
searchDocs.content: The menu bar at the top of the screen allows you to change display conditions and review detailed data.  Filter Filter data by referrer and other conditions. Clicking the button opens the filter window. Period Change the time range of the data displayed in the heatmap. Device Selection Switch between PC, Smartphone, and Tablet data. Update Heatmap Version Updates the HTML of the page used to render the heatmap.

searchDocs.title: Metrics​
searchDocs.content: Valid Data The number of sessions used to generate the heatmap. Average Stay The average time users spent viewing the page. This excludes time when the user was not actively viewing the page.

searchDocs.title: Notes​
searchDocs.content: Data Differences The number of page views and the data used to render the heatmap may differ because they use different aggregation criteria. Impact of Site Changes If the HTML of the page has changed, the heatmap may not perfectly match the current page content. For more details, see Heatmap Versions.

searchDocs.title: Realtime
searchDocs.keywords: QA ZERO  WordPress  analytics  heatmap  documentation

searchDocs.title: Realtime Overview​
searchDocs.content: The current site status is displayed using two metrics.

searchDocs.title: Users in the last 30 minutes​
searchDocs.content: This shows the total number of users who performed either a page entry or a page transition within the last 30 minutes.  If a user has no activity for 30 minutes after their last action, the session is considered complete and is no longer included in this count.

searchDocs.title: Active users in the last 1 minute​
searchDocs.content: This shows the number of users who performed a page entry or a page transition within the last 1 minute.  For example, users who opened a page but did not interact (e.g., switched to another tab or remained inactive) for over a minute are not counted.  This metric represents users who are actively viewing the site right now.

searchDocs.title: Session Recording (Video Playback)​
searchDocs.content: A list of recent user sessions is displayed (up to 5,000 sessions).  Only the most recent 5,000 sessions are available in Realtime. To view older sessions, use the Audience report, where you can select a specific period.

searchDocs.title: Use cases​
searchDocs.content: Check recent behavior Review sessions immediately after users leave the site. You can quickly see which pages they viewed and where they encountered issues. Monitor changes and campaigns After updating content or launching a campaign, use real user behavior data to verify whether users are interacting as expected.

searchDocs.title: How to Access Session Recordings​
searchDocs.content: You can open session recordings from the following menus:  RealtimeVisitsGoals  Click the play icon in the Replay column of each data list to open the player.

searchDocs.title: Playback Controls​
searchDocs.content: Use the controls at the bottom of the screen to manage playback.  Speed: 0.5x / 1x / 2x / 4xSeek: Move to any point using the progress bar

searchDocs.title: Session Information​
searchDocs.content: Detailed session data is displayed on the right side.  User Anonymous ID (assigned by QA ZERO)New / ReturningCountry Visit Start timeReferrerPage views Device Device type (Desktop / Mobile / Tablet)OSBrowser Viewed Pages Pages viewed in the same sessionPlayback automatically moves to the next page if multiple pages exist

searchDocs.title: Privacy Protection​
searchDocs.content: Session Recording does not capture user input.  The following data is automatically excluded:  Form inputs Personal data such as names, addresses, and phone numbers are not recorded. Confirmation pages Pages displaying submitted data are not recorded and cannot be viewed.  No additional masking configuration is required.
